company.name: Electronic Arts
company.description: Electronic Arts develops and publishes video games and live services across console, PC, and mobile platforms.

overview.title: Business profile
paragraphs: Electronic Arts is a global interactive-entertainment publisher built around owned studios, licensed sports franchises, recurring live-service monetization, and cross-platform distribution.
Its strongest economics come from large communities around annualized sports titles, extra content, and long-lived franchises rather than one-time packaged-game sales alone.

overview.title: Registry relevance
paragraphs: EA is a useful registry case because the product surface mixes creative IP, sports licensing, player identity, digital marketplaces, matchmaking, and modding constraints.

The most credible open alternatives are not direct one-for-one replacements for EA's production budget, but community-owned engines, federated virtual worlds, open content pipelines, and protocol-governed competition layers that reduce dependence on a single publisher.

moatNarrative: EA's moat is strong because its leading franchises combine brand recognition, sports and league licenses, proprietary content pipelines, large player communities, and accumulated live-service operations.

The moat is narrower at the engine and tooling layer, where open-source engines and community content ecosystems can keep improving, but it remains deep for polished annual sports simulation, official club/player rights, anti-cheat operations, and mass-market distribution.
decentralizationNarrative: EA's core games are centralized products: matchmaking, accounts, economy rules, content entitlements, telemetry, moderation, and live operations are controlled by the publisher.

Decentralization pressure is more plausible around creation and governance than around immediate AAA replacement: open engines, self-hosted worlds, federated identity, community-run competitions, and transparent content licensing can substitute for pieces of the stack before they substitute for the full franchise.
company.snapshotNote: Queued from the May 25, 2026 S&P 500 market-cap snapshot ranks 201-225.

moat.rationale: Major franchises, sports licenses, live-service scale, and recurring digital content revenue create a high moat even though open tools can pressure parts of the development stack.

decentralizability.rationale: Game creation tools, servers, communities, and mod ecosystems can decentralize, but EA's flagship products depend heavily on licensed IP, centralized accounts, matchmaking, economies, and anti-cheat operations.

profitability.rationale: EA remains profitable at large scale, though recent fiscal results show live-service dependence and meaningful development and operating costs rather than software-style margin dominance.

peRatio.rationale: Approximate market-cap-to-earnings ratio using May 2026 market capitalization near $50.6B and reported fiscal 2026 net income around $887M; this is a rough registry input rather than a live valuation feed.

marketCap.rationale: CompaniesMarketCap reported Electronic Arts market capitalization of about $50.61B as of May 2026.

products.name: EA Sports FC
products.summary: EA Sports FC is EA's association-football game franchise, continuing the company's long-running soccer simulation and Ultimate Team-style live-service model after the FIFA-branded era.
products.whyItMatters: The franchise concentrates official football licensing, annualized sports simulation, digital collectibles, matchmaking, esports-style competition, and global fan communities inside a publisher-controlled stack.
replacementSketch: A realistic replacement path starts with open simulation tools, community asset licensing, and self-hosted leagues rather than immediate parity with official club and player licenses.
Over time, federated competitions and transparent roster/content marketplaces could let clubs, creators, and players coordinate outside a single publisher account and economy.

alternatives.name: Godot Engine
alternatives.summary: Godot is a free, open-source, MIT-licensed 2D and 3D game engine that can support community-built sports games and modifiable simulation experiments.

openness.rationale: Godot is explicitly free and open source under the MIT license, with public source code and permissive reuse terms.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The engine does not decentralize a sports league by itself, but it lets independent teams fork, self-host, and build interoperable tooling without a proprietary engine owner.

readiness.rationale: Godot is production-capable for many 2D and 3D games, but matching EA Sports FC's animation fidelity, licensing, online scale, and anti-cheat stack remains a large project.

costLeverage.rationale: A permissive engine eliminates engine licensing costs and allows community reuse of tooling, prototypes, and learning materials.

disruptionConcepts.name: Federated Football League Stack
disruptionConcepts.summary: A federated football-game stack would separate simulation clients, league servers, roster data, club branding rights, tournaments, and player reputation so communities can run leagues without surrendering identity and economy control to one publisher.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: EA's strongest control point is not only the game client but the licensing, identity, competition, and economy layer around it; federating those layers would let independent clients and communities compete on gameplay while sharing portable leagues and reputations.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Federation matters more than Bitcoin here: independent league operators can host servers, publish rule sets, and interoperate through signed rosters and portable player identities instead of relying on one global EA account system.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Clubs, creators, and league operators publish signed season rules, rosters, match schedules, and moderation policies; clients connect to approved servers and players carry portable reputation across leagues.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Match results are signed by participating servers and clients, suspicious telemetry is auditable by league operators, and roster/asset provenance is checked against public registries; the weak point is still client tampering and collusion in high-stakes matches.
failureModes: Official football licenses may remain unavailable to open leagues.
Anti-cheat and animation quality may lag commercial AAA expectations.
Fragmented governance could split players across incompatible rule sets.
adoptionPath: Start with community and amateur clubs that can license their own marks directly.
Build open clients and server tooling around small tournaments before attempting mass-market annual releases.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Federated leagues directly move identity, competition, and governance away from a single publisher.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: The model is credible for community leagues, but coordination with clubs, players, and rights holders is materially harder than the software architecture.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Open engines reduce tooling barriers, but sports animation, online synchronization, and anti-cheat remain demanding.

incumbentPressure.rationale: This would pressure EA at the community and long-tail league layer first, not the official top-tier football-license business immediately.

disruptionConcepts.name: Lightning Prize Community Tournaments
disruptionConcepts.summary: Community-run football-game tournaments could use Lightning escrow and open tournament software to pay entry fees, bounties, and prize splits instantly across borders without a publisher-operated marketplace.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: If competitive communities can fund and settle tournaments directly, some value migrates from publisher-controlled live-service economies toward player- and organizer-operated competition markets.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Lightning is central as a low-fee settlement rail for small entry fees, streaming prizes, referee bounties, and creator payouts without requiring a game publisher to custody balances.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Tournament organizers post rules and prize terms, players escrow entry fees, match servers submit signed results, and payouts settle automatically when challenge windows close.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Escrow releases depend on server-signed match logs, player attestations, and dispute windows; cheating is constrained by reputation loss, referee review, and delayed payout for flagged matches, but not eliminated.
failureModes: Regulatory treatment of paid competitions varies by jurisdiction.
Cheat detection can become the central trust bottleneck.
The concept needs enough players in open football clients to matter.
adoptionPath: Begin with small free-to-enter or sponsor-funded tournaments for open sports prototypes.
Add optional Lightning prize pools after rules, anti-cheat review, and community moderation are proven.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Direct tournament settlement reduces dependence on a central publisher economy, though gameplay servers still need governance.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Escrowed tournaments are straightforward conceptually, but fair adjudication and anti-cheat are hard in competitive games.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Payment plumbing is feasible, while trustworthy match verification and player liquidity are the limiting factors.

incumbentPressure.rationale: This pressures ancillary esports and creator-economy layers more than the official EA Sports FC content business.

products.name: The Sims
products.summary: The Sims is EA's life-simulation franchise centered on character creation, household management, building, storytelling, downloadable content, and community creativity.
products.whyItMatters: The franchise is a major example of publisher-controlled creative play: user identity, official packs, marketplaces, updates, and moderation sit around a game category that naturally invites modding and community worlds.
replacementSketch: The most realistic replacement is not a single clone, but an open life-simulation and virtual-world stack where communities host worlds, define rules, and share interoperable assets.
Open engines and federated virtual-world servers can give creators more durable ownership of worlds, scripts, and social spaces than a centrally operated franchise.

alternatives.name: OpenSimulator
alternatives.summary: OpenSimulator is an open-source, multi-user 3D application server for creating customizable virtual worlds, including optionally connected Hypergrid worlds.

openness.rationale: OpenSimulator is described by its project as open source and extensible for virtual-world developers.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Its Hypergrid model can connect independently hosted worlds, making it structurally closer to a distributed metaverse than a single publisher service.

readiness.rationale: OpenSimulator is a mature virtual-world server, but it is not a polished Sims-style consumer game with EA-level art direction, onboarding, or simulation depth.

costLeverage.rationale: Self-hosted open infrastructure reduces platform lock-in and allows communities to reuse servers and content workflows.

alternatives.name: Luanti
alternatives.summary: Luanti, formerly Minetest, is an open-source voxel game creation platform with multiplayer, modding, and a large ecosystem of community content.

openness.rationale: Luanti describes itself as an open-source engine and creation platform with transparent development and public source code.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Players and creators can host servers, make games, publish mods, and customize worlds without relying on a single publisher service.

readiness.rationale: Luanti is ready for voxel worlds and community games, but only partially overlaps with Sims-style life simulation and social storytelling.

costLeverage.rationale: Free engine, modding APIs, self-hosting, and reusable open content lower experimentation costs for community simulation games.

disruptionConcepts.name: Federated Life-Sim Worlds
disruptionConcepts.summary: A federated life-simulation network would let players keep avatars, homes, objects, scripts, and social reputations across independently hosted worlds instead of depending on one publisher's entitlement and update system.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: The Sims' creative moat rests partly on official packs and a centralized product cadence; federated worlds would shift value toward persistent communities, portable assets, and locally governed simulation spaces.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Federation is the central mechanism: worlds can be hosted by families, schools, creators, or communities while shared protocols keep identity, inventory metadata, and travel between worlds interoperable.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: World operators publish server rules, asset policies, and compatibility manifests; creators publish objects and scripts; players join worlds that accept their identity and inventory credentials.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Assets are signed by creators, worlds whitelist accepted script permissions, and reputation follows identities across servers; risks remain around malicious scripts, copied assets, and inconsistent moderation.
failureModes: Consumer polish and onboarding may trail EA's official product experience.
Interoperable asset licensing is legally and socially difficult.
Federated moderation can fragment safety expectations across worlds.
adoptionPath: Start with creator and education communities already comfortable hosting virtual worlds.
Add Sims-like household simulation modules, object catalogs, and portable identity once basic world interoperability works.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The model directly replaces a single operated creative world with many independently governed worlds.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: OpenSimulator already documents multi-user worlds and Hypergrid-style connectivity, though a Sims-like gameplay layer would need new standards.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: The virtual-world substrate exists, but high-quality life simulation, content safety, and consumer UX remain substantial work.

incumbentPressure.rationale: This pressures creator lock-in and long-tail community worlds more than EA's polished official expansion-pack business in the near term.

disruptionConcepts.name: Open Creator Pack Market
disruptionConcepts.summary: An open creator-pack market would let artists, builders, and modders publish interoperable Sims-like objects, rooms, avatars, and scripts under transparent licenses, with optional peer-to-peer payments and community curation.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: EA monetizes much of the life-sim experience through official content packs; open creator packs could unbundle creative content from publisher-controlled stores and make creator reputation portable.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Bitcoin or Lightning can be useful but not mandatory: micropayments can support small creator purchases or tips without a central store, while decentralized licensing and reputation are the more important structural changes.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Creators publish signed pack manifests, communities curate compatibility lists, users install packs through open clients, and optional payments or donations route directly to creators.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Packs are hash-addressed, signed, scanned in sandboxes, and reviewed by communities; cheating is less central than malware, asset theft, and license misrepresentation.
failureModes: Copyright disputes over copied commercial assets could undermine trust.
Malicious scripts or adult content create moderation burdens.
Fragmented formats could prevent broad reuse.
adoptionPath: Standardize a simple object and room-pack manifest for open engines and virtual worlds.
Layer in reputation, license metadata, and optional direct creator payments after basic safety review tooling exists.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Creator packs move content supply and curation away from a single publisher store toward many creators and communities.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Modding and community content distribution are already natural fits for open game platforms, but licensing and safety workflows need discipline.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Pack manifests, signing, and curation are feasible; high-quality creation tools and moderation are the hard parts.

incumbentPressure.rationale: A strong open creator economy would pressure official pack pricing and creator lock-in, though EA's brand and polish remain major advantages.
